claim.text: The decimal numerals and zero were invented by Arab mathematicians ('Arabic numerals').
claim.asCirculated: "Zero was invented by al-Khwarizmi" / "Arabic numerals, an Arab gift to the world".

whatsTrue: The Arab world's role was enormous and real: al-Khwārizmī's treatise carried the system to Baghdad and thence (in Latin translation) to Europe; 'algorithm' descends from his name; Islamic mathematicians extended the system (decimal fractions, algebra). Europe genuinely received the numerals from Arabic sources — the European name is honest about the messenger.
whatsDistorted: Origin: the numerals (Brahmi → Gupta → Nāgarī glyph lineage), decimal place value, and zero-as-number are Indian, and the Arabic tradition said so itself — al-Khwārizmī's book is 'On Calculation with HINDU Numerals' (c. 825); the Arabic term is ḥisāb al-hind, 'Indian reckoning'; Severus Sebokht already praised the Indians' 'nine signs' in 662 CE, before the Abbasids existed.

supporting.point: Transmission to Europe was via the Arabic world; major mathematical development happened there.

sources.label: Berggren, J.L., Episodes in the Mathematics of Medieval Islam (Springer, 1986)

countering.point: Arabic primary sources attribute the system to India by name; Syriac attestation predates the Arabic reception.

sources.label: al-Khwārizmī, Kitāb al-ḥisāb al-hindī (c. 825; Latin: Algoritmi de numero Indorum)
Severus Sebokht (662 CE), fragment on Indian numerals (ed. F. Nau, 1910)
Burnett, C., "The Semantics of Indian Numerals...", J. Indian Philosophy 34 (2006)

verdict.rationale: A transmission chain read backwards. The precise statement credits both: Indian invention, Arab development and transmission — which is exactly what the medieval Arabic mathematicians themselves wrote.

sources.label: Nau, F., "Notes d'astronomie syrienne" (1910) — Sebokht text
Berggren, J.L., Episodes in the Mathematics of Medieval Islam (1986)

quickRebuttal: The medieval Arabic mathematicians themselves credited India: al-Khwārizmī's book is 'On Calculation with HINDU Numerals' (c. 825), the Arabic term is ḥisāb al-hind ('Indian reckoning'), and Bishop Severus Sebokht praised the Indians' 'nine signs' in 662 CE — before the Abbasids existed. Indian invention, Arab development and transmission: credit both, as the sources do. (Burnett 2006; Chrisomalis 2010)

spread.note: Name-inertia ('Arabic numerals') does most of the work; textbook shorthand keeps it alive.
knownVariants: Arabic numerals — an Arab gift to mathematics
